All reports
NEXQLOUD SEALED

Competitive Intelligence Report

The battlefield map: where every rival screens at a gateway or seals the room, and where the per-action receipt they structurally lack opens the lane.

Prepared by Fenn and Hart
June 2026

0. Executive Summary

Bottom line. Every named rival has converged on the same governance words and ceded the same proof. Guardrail vendors (Lakera, Protect AI, HiddenLayer) screen text at a software gateway and emit a console log. Confidential-compute vendors (Anjuna, Fortanix) and the hyperscaler (Microsoft Azure) seal the room the model runs in and attest the platform, then assign per-action agent governance and the audit trail to the buyer. The one artifact none of them ships is a hardware-signed, per-action receipt a regulator can verify without contacting the company, spanning the closed third-party model the operator does not control. That artifact is the only lane a competitor cannot copy with a memo. Sealed’s exposure is that the artifact is real but unproven in production, and the proof a regulated buyer can check today belongs to the parent company, not to Sealed.

Where Sealed wins on the structure of the claim: the per-action, independently verifiable receipt across the closed-model boundary. Anjuna’s homepage now reads “Autonomous Agents Need a Supervisor,”1 Lakera’s banner reads “Controlling what AI can access isn’t enough. Control what it does,”2 and Fortanix sells “cryptographic guarantees, not contractual ones.”3 The vocabulary has collapsed into one shared script. The receipt is where the script runs out for all of them.

Where Sealed ties: data-in-use protection. Azure, Fortanix, and Anjuna all run real hardware trusted execution environments (TEEs) with platform attestation.4 5 6 Sealed does not out-claim them on confidentiality of the box. The divergence is what sits above the box.

Where Sealed lags today: proof maturity and brand surface. Sealed is at design-partner stage with no production cohort. The publicly verifiable SOC 2 is the parent Distributed Cloud Platform’s Type 1 (announced November 13, 2025), not a Sealed Type II, and the publicly announced patents are six parent Cloud-OS filings from September 2025, filed not granted.7 8 A regulated buyer running a security review finds the parent’s credentials, not Sealed’s.

The single biggest exposure to defend against: a rival who reframes the conversation as “governance” generically, where the language is now identical and the buyer cannot tell the products apart. The counter is to move the conversation to the artifact every time.

The single biggest open lane: the regulator-forwardable per-action receipt across the closed-model boundary. On public evidence, no named competitor produces it. The lane is open. The work is to ship the production reference that proves it.

1. Competitor Matrix and the Sealed Snapshot

Figure 1. Capability presence per competitor, grouped by camp. Filled = has it, half = partial, open = none. Use it on sales calls when a prospect asks how you compare to a named rival. Every rival is filled somewhere. Only Sealed holds the per-action receipt across the closed-model boundary.

The six competitors split into two camps that fail the same buyer question from opposite directions. The guardrail camp screens at a software gateway and writes a log. The confidential-compute camp seals the execution environment and attests the platform. Neither produces a per-action receipt a third party can verify independently.

Competitor Camp Verbatim H1 / lead line Real durable power Hardware-signed per-action receipt Governs the closed-model boundary with proof Ownership status
Lakera Guardrail / prompt-firewall “The leading security platform to secure your AI future”9 Cornered Resource (Gandalf 80M+ adversarial prompts)10 No (API verdict, “business logic”)11 No (screens text beside the model)12 Acquired by Check Point, announced Sep 16, 202513
Protect AI AI/ML security (MLSecOps) “The Platform for AI Security”14 Network Economies (huntr 17,000+ researchers, Hugging Face)15 No (software “audit trail” in own console)16 No (scans and monitors only)17 Acquired by Palo Alto Networks, completed Jul 22, 202518
HiddenLayer AI detection-and-response “The most comprehensive security platform for AI”19 Counter-Positioning + research IP (30+ patents, 50+ CVEs)20 No (software alert context, console-resident)21 No (observes from outside the inference path)22 Private; $50M Series A, Sep 19, 202323
Microsoft Azure Hyperscaler confidential AI + contract “Increase data privacy by protecting data in use”24 Scale, Switching Costs, Branding25 No native product (Confidential Ledger is a build-it-yourself store)26 No (attests the platform; governance assigned to buyer)27 Incumbent hyperscaler
Anjuna Confidential compute (TEE virtualization) “Autonomous Agents Need a Supervisor”1 Cornered Resource (cross-chip TEE team) + Switching Costs28 No (Overwatch ships no receipt or log; case studies empty)29 No (cannot enclave a closed external model)30 Private; $25M Series B2, Aug 7, 202431
Fortanix Confidential compute + key/data security “AI Innovation Without Fear of Exposure”32 Counter-Positioning (data-first) + Switching Costs (key custody)33 No (“immutable audit log,” not a per-action receipt)34 No (brings the model into the customer’s own TEE)35 Private; $90M Series C, Sep 15, 202236

1.1 The Sealed Snapshot

  1. Positioning. NexQloud Sealed leads on the proof artifact: a hardware-signed, independently verifiable per-action receipt that lets a regulator confirm what an organization’s AI did, action by action, across the whole boundary including closed third-party models, without contacting the company (BLF-007).
  2. Target. A dual buyer set: the regulated organization that owns the data and the liability (healthcare system, bank, insurer, law firm, government contractor with a board mandate and a named compliance owner), and the AI vendor stalled at a security review. The wedge into both is the same: provable per-action governance that survives a real security review under a forcing event (BLF-005, BLF-006).
  3. Claimed differentiators. Per-action governance with cryptographic proof across the closed-model boundary (BLF-003, BLF-007); governing closed models rather than refusing them, by attesting both sides of the boundary without asserting what happens inside the model (BLF-004, BLF-010); a claims-discipline voice that states what it cannot prove (BLF-008).
  4. Visible strengths. A structural architecture rivals cannot replicate with positioning: hardware root of trust, sealed-enclave execution, a public transparency log, and filed IP. The parent’s third-party SOC 2 Type 1 and FedRAMP Moderate ambition give partial institutional credibility.7
  • Visible weaknesses, stated plainly. Design-partner stage, pre-revenue, no production cohort (BLF-011). The Sealed patents are filed and still awaiting grant. The publicly verifiable SOC 2 belongs to the parent (Type 1), while a Sealed-named Type II is not yet surfaced. The live verifier and the disciplined governance story sit under a separate brand (NexArt), away from the buyer-facing Sealed surface (ARG-2). The proof asset is invisible at the exact moment it is meant to close the deal.

2. Per-Competitor Deep Dives

Each dive renders one competitor’s full dossier. The order runs guardrail-camp first (Lakera, Protect AI, HiddenLayer), then the hyperscaler (Microsoft Azure), then the confidential-compute camp (Anjuna, Fortanix). Each ends with the open lane that sits next to that specific competitor, so a rep reading a single card sees the opportunity without flipping to the Whitespace Map.

2.1 Lakera (a Check Point company): the data moat that stops at the prompt boundary

  1. URL and verbatim positioning. https://www.lakera.ai/ . Homepage H1: “The leading security platform to secure your AI future.”9 The AI Agent Security page states verbatim that the product “enforces security on every AI interaction your organization runs by inspecting what goes in, controlling what comes out, and governing what your agents do. It deploys in minutes, requires no changes to your models or prompts, and adds no meaningful latency.”37 The architecture, in Lakera’s own words on the security page: “LLM inputs and outputs are transmitted via our API, where they’re immediately screened for potential threats using proprietary AI models and business logic.”11
  2. Real strategic posture (Helmer’s 7 Powers). Lakera holds exactly one durable, real Power: a Cornered Resource. The Gandalf game has produced “80M+ Total Prompts” and “1M+ Total Players,” and the Check Point acquisition release credits “Gandalf’s 80 million-plus adversarial patterns.”10 This is a genuine, hard-to-replicate red-team corpus, and it is the single most defensible thing Lakera owns. It is a moat on prompt-attack detection. It confers no advantage in cryptographic proof, attestation, or sealed execution. Branding was real and mid-strength (Gandalf made Lakera the recognizable name in prompt injection), and it is now being absorbed into Check Point’s brand. Scale Economies are borrowed from the parent rather than built by Lakera. Switching Costs are weak by Lakera’s own design: “deploys in minutes, requires no changes to your models or prompts” is a low-friction selling point that also means low friction to rip out.38 Counter-Positioning against “traditional security” is category-generic and now self-cancelling, because Lakera sits inside Check Point, a traditional-security incumbent.
  3. Target and scope. Fortune 500 and large regulated enterprises deploying GenAI, LLM apps, and autonomous agents, including banking (Nubank) and healthcare (Hinge Health). Three products: Workforce AI Security (shadow-AI discovery), AI Agent Security (runtime protection, formerly Lakera Guard), AI Red Teaming (formerly Lakera Red), plus Gandalf for training. Deployment is cloud SaaS via API plus a self-hosted container option.
  4. Claimed differentiators (verbatim). “Reduce risks by 3-4 orders of magnitude,” “sub-50 ms runtime latency,” “0.01% production false positive rate,” “100+ languages supported,” “Multimodal and model agnostic.”39
  5. Visible strengths. Roughly $30M raised before acquisition (a $10M seed led by Redalpine around October 2023, a $20M Series A led by Atomico on July 24, 2024), then a reported ~$300M Check Point acquisition.40 Named live logos: Dropbox, Pearson, Hinge Health, Nubank. Recognition in the Gartner 2024 Innovation Guide for GenAI and the OWASP LLM and GenAI Security Landscape Guide 2025.10
  6. Visible weaknesses. The security page describes protection delivered by transmitting inputs and outputs “via our API” for screening, a software gateway with no mention of enclave execution, remote attestation, or a signed per-action receipt.11 Lakera sits beside the model as a filter; the model still runs wherever it ran before. Protection is probabilistic by Lakera’s own numbers: a stated “0.01% production false positive rate” and, per the Check Point release, “detection rates above 98 percent… false positives below 0.5 percent.”41 An ML classifier admits a nonzero miss rate and issues an allow-or-block verdict, not court-grade proof. The live compliance surface is thin: SOC 2 and GDPR only, with no ISO 27001 or FedRAMP shown.42
  7. Recent dated activity. Check Point announced its agreement to acquire Lakera on September 16, 2025, for a reported ~$300M, expected to close Q4 2025; the live Lakera.ai footer now reads “©1994-2026 Check Point Software Technologies Ltd.”13 The widely repeated claim that Cisco acquired Lakera in May 2025 is false; Cisco acquired the separate competitor Robust Intelligence.13
  8. Overlap with Sealed. Lakera uses Sealed’s own lead vocabulary almost word for word. The homepage banner reads “Controlling what AI can access isn’t enough. Control what it does,” and the product copy reads “by inspecting what goes in, controlling what comes out, and governing what your agents do.”2 That phrase can no longer differentiate Sealed against a Fortune-500-proofed rival with an 80M-prompt data moat.

The open lane next to Lakera: Lakera governs the text crossing the wire and produces an API verdict backed by “business logic.” It runs no model in a sealed enclave and issues no hardware-signed, tamper-evident receipt per action. The lane Sealed occupies is the execution environment and the proof artifact, neither of which any Lakera Power touches. Sealed’s wedge against Lakera lands on the artifact: “we hand a regulator a receipt for each action, and Lakera’s verdict is a probabilistic filter that cannot.”

2.2 Protect AI (now Prisma AIRS): breadth-of-coverage, with a log where the receipt should be

  1. URL and verbatim positioning. https://protectai.com . Homepage H1: “The Platform for AI Security.”14 Subhead, verbatim: “Protect AI is the broadest and most comprehensive AI security solution. Our suite of products, Guardian, Recon, and Layer, operate on a single, unified platform and secure AI applications from model selection and testing to runtime and beyond.”43 A persistent banner across all pages reads “A Prisma AIRS Power Play! Palo Alto Networks Completes Acquisition of Protect AI.”
  2. Real strategic posture (Helmer’s 7 Powers). As an independent company, Protect AI’s only durable Powers were Network Economies and a partial Cornered Resource, both built on threat intelligence. The huntr bug-bounty community (“17,000+ security researchers”), the MLSecOps community (“8,000+ members”), and the Hugging Face scanning partnership form a real threat-intel flywheel.15 Both now belong to Palo Alto Networks. The Counter-Positioning that came from creating the MLSecOps category collapsed the moment Protect AI became the incumbent it had positioned against. Switching Costs are weak: scanning and red-teaming are project-based and replaceable. There is no original Power in cryptographic proof, hardware attestation, or sealed execution anywhere in the portfolio.
  3. Target and scope. Enterprise security and ML/AI engineering teams in regulated and large industries, “finance, government, and healthcare to manufacturing and retail.” Scope runs the full AI lifecycle: model files (Guardian scans “35+ different model formats”), deployed app endpoints (Recon, “450+ known attacks”), and runtime traffic (Layer, “27 turnkey policies… 15 different security scanners,” eBPF or SDK).44 The buyer is the CISO, now sold inside the Palo Alto platform consolidation.
  4. Claimed differentiators (verbatim). “the broadest and most comprehensive AI security solution”; “the widest and deepest set of model scanners on the market”; Layer: “Our models detect attack patterns that competitors miss.”45
  5. Visible strengths. Roughly $108.5M raised before acquisition (Seed $13.5M December 15, 2022; Series A $35M July 26, 2023; Series B $60M August 1, 2024).46 A wall of award badges (Fortune Cyber 60, CB Insights AI 100). Named customers and partners: Leidos, TELUS Digital, Hugging Face, AWS, Databricks, Microsoft.
  6. Visible weaknesses. Every product is software interception: static file scanning, dynamic adversarial testing, eBPF or SDK traffic monitoring, with no confidential computing or TEE attestation in the stack.17 Guardian markets “a comprehensive audit trail of all evaluations in your centralized Guardian environment,” which is an application log held inside Protect AI’s own console, not a hardware-attested, independently verifiable receipt.16 Layer relies on detection with an irreducible bypass surface.47 The standalone protectai.com is a zombie site: products are “no longer sold separately” post-acquisition, yet the page still solicits “Request a demo,” and the “Explore the Latest” feed surfaces posts dated January to April 2025.48
  7. Recent dated activity. Palo Alto Networks announced intent to acquire on April 28, 2025, and completed the acquisition on July 22, 2025, folding Protect AI into Prisma AIRS; CEO Ian Swanson joined as VP, Product, Prisma AIRS.18 Acquisition terms were not officially disclosed; industry reports estimated “north of $500 million.”46
  8. Overlap with Sealed. Protect AI is the canonical example of the surface vocabulary Sealed must not be lumped into: “audit trail,” “runtime,” “policies” mapped to “NIST, MITRE, and OWASP,” “end-to-end.” It screens, scans, monitors, and logs.49

The open lane next to Protect AI: Protect AI’s strongest government quote, from Leidos, frames the win as “from the model file to runtime behavior… a holistic, platform-based approach.”50 That is coverage breadth, and it never asserts proof of what the AI did per action, even in the regulated and national-security contexts Leidos serves. In those exact contexts the unanswered question is whether a buyer can prove, hardware-rooted and tamper-evidently, what the AI did on each action. The Prisma AIRS stack answers with logs. Sealed answers with a receipt.

2.3 HiddenLayer: the rival that argues Sealed’s case in its own blog

  1. URL and verbatim positioning. https://www.hiddenlayer.com/ . Homepage H1: “The most comprehensive security platform for AI.”19 The platform secures “agentic, generative, and predictive AI applications across the entire lifecycle” through four modules: AI Discovery, AI Supply Chain Security, AI Attack Simulation, AI Runtime Security.51 The runtime page reads: “Detect and Stop AI Attacks in Real Time… HiddenLayer protects agents from prompt attacks, jailbreaks, unsafe outputs, and malicious tool use.”52
  2. Real strategic posture (Helmer’s 7 Powers). HiddenLayer’s two durable Powers are Counter-Positioning and a Cornered Resource. The Counter-Positioning is genuine: “Purpose-Built for AI Security… not retrofitted from traditional cybersecurity,” a lane incumbent EDR and SIEM vendors cannot follow without cannibalizing.53 The Cornered Resource is its research IP: “30+ Issued patents,” “50+ CVEs Disclosed through our security research,” and a threat-research team behind work like ShadowLogic.20 That resource is eroding as Palo Alto (via Protect AI) and Cisco (via Robust Intelligence) field competing research. The remaining Powers are claimed or absent. Brand is young (founded March 2022) and “most comprehensive” is an assertion. With roughly 167 to 169 employees and $55.8M raised, HiddenLayer holds no Scale Economies against hyperscaler-backed rivals.54
  3. Target and scope. Buyer roles named in the nav: CISO, AI Leaders, Application Developers. Industries: Financial Services, Technology, and US Federal Government (“Defend mission-critical AI systems with security aligned to national standards”). The deployment claim, verbatim: “Model-Agnostic, Agentless, Zero Training Data Required… Deploy in minutes across any architecture without exposing intellectual property, weights, prompts, or customer data.”22
  4. Claimed differentiators (verbatim). “Purpose-Built for AI Security”; “Model-Agnostic, Agentless”; AISec Platform 2.0 (April 22, 2025) added Model Genealogy (“lineage and pedigree of AI models”) and AIBOM (“an auditable inventory of model components, datasets, and dependencies”).55
  5. Visible strengths. A $50M Series A on September 19, 2023, led by M12 (Microsoft’s Venture Fund) and Moore Strategic Ventures, with Booz Allen, IBM, and Capital One Ventures participating, the largest Series A by an AI-security company that year.23 CISO endorsements from the NFL (Tomas Maldonado) and GitLab (Josh Lemos). Federal positioning with DoD and CDAO seals on the logo wall.
  6. Visible weaknesses. The entire evidence model is software-generated alert context, with no TPM, secure enclave, or hardware-signed artifact.21 The agentless design is the explicit opposite of sealed execution: HiddenLayer “observes from outside” and never runs the model, so it cannot attest what happens inside a closed third-party model.22 The runtime product is an “AI Guardrails & Firewall” that enforces guardrails and blocks malicious prompts, software-gateway screening that emits alerts.56 The compliance footprint on-site is SOC 2 only, with no FedRAMP or hardware attestation.57
  7. Recent dated activity. AISec Platform 2.0 launched at RSAC on April 22, 2025.55 In 2026 HiddenLayer released a “2026 AI Threat Landscape Report” arguing “AI systems are no longer just generating outputs, they are taking action,” and a June 5, 2026 blog on NSPM-11 elevating AI security to a national-security requirement.58
  8. Overlap with Sealed. Both invoke governance, compliance, agentic AI taking action, real-time, and the closed-model risk. Both target regulated industries and US Federal. Sealed must not read as a fourth module of HiddenLayer’s lifecycle pitch.

The open lane next to HiddenLayer: HiddenLayer makes Sealed’s argument out loud. Its own insight reads, “Detection without evidence does not reduce risk, it shifts the burden downstream,” then defines “evidence” as “the exact function calls associated with a detection, the arguments passed into those functions, the configurations.”21 All of it is software-derived, console-resident, and vendor-attested, with no cryptographic signature and no independently verifiable public log. Its June 2026 NSPM-11 post insists “trust requires verification” and that assurance must “operate independently of any single model vendor,” yet the verifier it offers is its own console.59 HiddenLayer has named the exact gap and left it open. Sealed fills it with a hardware-signed receipt in a public log.

2.4 Microsoft Azure: the incumbent that documents the gap it leaves the buyer

  1. Scope of this dive. Azure is profiled as the bundle a regulated buyer would assemble to approximate Sealed: Azure Confidential Computing (confidential VMs and GPUs), Azure Confidential Ledger, Azure OpenAI / “Models sold by Azure,” Microsoft Purview DSPM for AI, and Azure AI Content Safety. The question throughout is what Azure provides natively versus what the buyer must still build.
  2. URL and verbatim positioning. https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/solutions/confidential-compute . The H1: “Azure confidential computing: Increase data privacy by protecting data in use.”24 Verbatim: confidential computing “encrypts data in memory in hardware-based trusted execution environments and processes it only after the cloud environment is verified. This helps prevent data access by cloud providers, administrators, and users.”24 Nowhere on the marketing surface does Azure claim per-action AI agent governance or a per-action audit receipt.
  3. Real strategic posture (Helmer’s 7 Powers). Azure’s durable Powers are formidable and real for infrastructure: Scale Economies (a hyperscale fleet, the “first cloud provider with confidential H100 GPUs,” a stated $20B confidential-computing investment), Switching Costs (the Azure tenant, Entra ID, the DPA, the Microsoft 365 and Purview E5 estate), and Branding (a trust brand regulated buyers default to). None of those Powers extends to vendor-independent, per-action, regulator-forwardable governance across the closed-model boundary. Azure’s own shared-responsibility documentation assigns that job to the buyer. On governance specifically, the trust model is “trust Microsoft plus the contract,” which substitutes vendor reputation for vendor-independent proof.
  4. Target and scope. Regulated and security-sensitive enterprises and public-sector orgs already on Azure that want data-in-use protection plus a contractual AI privacy guarantee, typically through the Microsoft 365 E5, Foundry, and Purview stack.
  5. Claimed differentiators (verbatim). “Azure is the first cloud provider to offer confidential computing with NVIDIA H100 GPUs”; “Specify the hardware and software that have access to your data and code, and verifiably enforce this guarantee”; the Foundry commitment that prompts, completions, embeddings, and training data “are NOT used to train any generative AI foundation models without your permission or instruction.”60 37
  6. Visible strengths. Hardware data-in-use isolation (AMD SEV-SNP, Intel TDX, confidential H100 GPU with a TEE spanning CPU and GPU). Platform attestation that gates VM boot on SEV-SNP/TDX and boot measurements.4 A contractual, DPA-backed data-protection commitment that regulators recognize. Confidential Ledger, which mints Merkle-proof receipts.26 The broadest compliance-certification footprint of any vendor in this set.
  7. Visible weaknesses for this use case. Five gaps, all documented on first-party Microsoft pages. First, governance and the audit trail are left to the buyer: the AI shared-responsibility doc states an “application safety system must be built” by the customer, and that inspecting “plugins, data connectors, and other AI applications (known as AI Orchestration)” is the customer’s job.27 Second, single-vendor trust concentration: the default confidential-VM key posture is Microsoft-managed “for increased convenience,” and abuse monitoring lets “authorized Microsoft employees” access a sample of prompts and completions unless the customer is approved for modified abuse monitoring.61 62 Third, platform attestation verifies SEV-SNP/TDX and boot state, not the behavior of the closed model or the content of an individual agent action.4 Fourth, no regulator-forwardable per-action receipt out of the box: Confidential Ledger can mint Merkle receipts, but the buyer must design what to log, wire it to agent actions, and build the forwarding mechanism.26 Fifth, Purview DSPM for AI is posture and observability (E5-gated, M365-leaning), not real-time per-action allow-or-block with a cryptographic receipt.63
  8. Recent dated activity. GA of Azure confidential VMs with NVIDIA H100 GPUs (NCC H100 v5), East US2 and West Europe, announced September 2024.5 Enhanced Purview DSPM for AI across the Ignite 2024 to 2025 cycle, treating agents as “first-class entities,” in public preview and E5-gated.63 The Foundry data-privacy doc expanded to “Models sold by Azure” (page last updated May 19, 2026).37
  9. Overlap with Sealed. Both target regulated buyers who want to use closed AI on sensitive data without exposing it in use, both invoke cryptographic proof (Azure via Confidential Ledger Merkle receipts), and both address closed-model usage (Azure via the OpenAI contractual commitments plus isolation). The overlap is on the primitive; the divergence is on the product shape.

The open lane next to Azure: Microsoft, in its own words, concedes the layer Sealed productizes. The shared-responsibility doc says the application safety system “must be built” by the customer, that orchestration and plugin inspection are the customer’s responsibility, and that accountability for AI output rests increasingly on the user.27 Azure supplies the isolation hardware, platform attestation, a contractual promise, and a cryptographic-receipt primitive. The per-action agent governance layer and a vendor-independent, regulator-forwardable audit trail across the closed-model boundary are explicitly the buyer’s to build. Sealed’s lane is to build it once, vendor-independent, and portable across commodity GPUs and multiple clouds rather than bound to Azure’s H100 confidential SKUs in two regions.

2.5 Anjuna: the closest live encroacher, positioning ahead of its proof

  1. URL and verbatim positioning. https://www.anjuna.io/ . Homepage H1 (fetched June 18, 2026): “Autonomous Agents Need a Supervisor,” sub “Give them one in trusted environments.”1 The body reads: “Agentic AI introduces a new kind of risk… Anjuna’s cloud data security and privacy preserving AI becomes the incorruptible supervisor, isolating agents in TEEs, enforcing policy at runtime, and preventing unauthorized actions or data exposure.”1 As of this fetch Anjuna’s front door has shifted from “confidential computing platform” to “agentic AI security,” a direct move onto Sealed’s lane, with near-total shared vocabulary: supervisor, runtime policy, unauthorized actions, agentic AI, attestation, governance.
  2. Real strategic posture (Helmer’s 7 Powers). Anjuna’s held, durable Powers are a moderate Cornered Resource and moderate Switching Costs. The Cornered Resource is the founding team (ex-Unit 8200 intelligence officers) and the cross-chip, cross-cloud TEE virtualization stack (SGX, SEV-SNP, TDX, Nitro, H100 under one runtime), genuinely hard engineering shipped as Seaglass, the “first Universal Confidential Computing Platform,” in November 2023.28 Switching Costs come from wrapping apps as Confidential Containers and authoring attestation policies. The “agentic supervisor” branding is new and demo-stage, with no agent-governance case study live.
  3. Target and scope. Verticals named live: Financial Services, Government, Blockchain and Web3, Healthcare, Software Providers. Clouds: AWS (Nitro), Azure, Google Cloud, on-prem. Chips: SGX and SEV-SNP today, TDX and H100 as available. The motion is enterprise and government (“Contact Sales,” “Demo Request”) with a 30-day “Start Free” trial on top.
  4. Claimed differentiators (verbatim). “the first Universal Confidential Computing Platform to run any application in any major cloud”; “no code changes required”; Overwatch: “Establishes a trusted supervisor that governs autonomous agents in real time. Anjuna Overwatch verifies every request through hardware-rooted attestation, enforcing policies and controlling tool or data access based on context.”64
  5. Visible strengths. A funding ladder of a $30M Series B (Insight Partners, June 23, 2021) and a $25M Series B2 (M Ventures, SineWave, AI Capital Partners, August 7, 2024).31 Hard government and financial proof: the U.S. Navy tested Llama3 on confidential NVIDIA H100 GPUs (“confidential environments in less than one hour”), and on June 11, 2025 Anjuna announced it is “Now Trusted by Three of the World’s 10 Largest Banks.”65 Federal channel partners: Carahsoft, Leidos, SAIC, Lockheed Martin.
  6. Visible weaknesses. Overwatch has no live product page, no datasheet, and no case study; its agentic page reads “No current matching case studies. Check back soon.”29 The proof story across every page is attestation of code identity at boot plus runtime policy enforcement, with no per-action tamper-evident receipt and no external verifiable log.66 Verification runs against Anjuna’s own policy manager inside the customer’s cloud, so trust collapses to Anjuna plus the cloud. A closed external model the operator does not control cannot be placed inside Anjuna’s enclave.30 Some on-page efficiency stats are unsourced (“12% reduction in security spending,” “up to 90%/30%/50%”).
  7. Recent dated activity. “Anjuna Now Trusted by Three of the World’s 10 Largest Banks” (June 11, 2025); named to Fast Company “Most Innovative Companies 2025”; homepage repositioned to “Autonomous Agents Need a Supervisor” and the Overwatch narrative launched in the late-2025 to 2026 window.31
  8. Overlap with Sealed. Anjuna is the competitor whose words most closely shadow Sealed’s: supervisor, runtime policy, governing agent actions, isolate agents, MCP servers, preventing unauthorized actions. The surface language is nearly identical, which raises the bar: differentiation cannot rest on vocabulary; it must rest on the artifact produced.

The open lane next to Anjuna: Anjuna gates at boot and attestation and enforces runtime policy, so the unit of governance is the enclave and the identity rather than a discrete, individually authorized agent action. It runs the agent inside a TEE it controls, so a closed external model like GPT-4o cannot be enclaved and falls outside Anjuna’s trust boundary. And it ships no hardware-signed, append-only, externally published record a regulator can replay without contacting Anjuna. The sharpest one-line gap: Anjuna secures the room the agent runs in and attests the code at the door; it does not hand a regulator a hardware-signed, independently replayable receipt for each action the agent took, and it cannot enclave a closed model, so the closed-model boundary is where its trust story ends and Sealed’s begins.

2.6 Fortanix: cryptographic guarantees for the data, an immutable log for the action

  1. URL and verbatim positioning. https://www.fortanix.com/ . Homepage title: “Securing Data in an AI world.” The hero H1: “AI Innovation Without Fear of Exposure,” sub “Secure AI models, prompts, and data in use with Confidential Computing for trust, security and sovereignty.”32 The “Why Fortanix” claim: “Fortanix is the only data security provider that can truly say your data is under your control. Our platform lets you take back full custody of your cloud keys, and confidential computing technology ensures granular access controls for management and data access.”67 The Confidential AI page H1: “Deploy Frontier AI Where Data Lives.”35 Fortanix’s whole frame is data and model protection, where “control” means key custody and access control.
  2. Real strategic posture (Helmer’s 7 Powers). Fortanix’s durable Powers are Counter-Positioning and Switching Costs, discussed in plain terms. The Counter-Positioning is a real “data-first approach, decoupling security from infrastructure,” a genuine structural stance against perimeter-led incumbents.33 The Switching Costs are equally real and equally outside Sealed’s lane: once an enterprise’s keys, HSM custody, and encryption policy live in Data Security Manager (FIPS 140-2 Level 3 hardware, BYOK, AWS External Key Store, Google EKM wired into the hyperscaler clouds), migration is painful.34 Branding (“pioneer of Confidential Computing,” Gartner 4.6 as of January 2026) and a patent-backed Cornered Resource (“100+ security patents”) are real and contestable. None of these Powers extends into per-action agent governance.
  3. Target and scope. “More than 125 marquee global customers” across healthcare, banking, FSI, tech, manufacturing, and federal agencies, plus “several U.S. government agencies.” Roles: CxOs, Security Teams, Data Teams, AppDev Teams. The buyer is the highly regulated or sovereign enterprise needing data sovereignty, key custody, and compliance (GDPR, HIPAA, PCI DSS, DORA).
  4. Claimed differentiators (verbatim). “Protect data as it’s being used in addition to at-rest and in-transit”; Confidential AI “keeps both sensitive data and model IP encrypted and inaccessible to the underlying infrastructure”; “Composite attestation for CPU and GPU create a single chain of trust. Verify exactly what AI workload is running with hardware-signed proof”; and the sharpest line, from the March 2026 release, “Both parties operate with cryptographic guarantees, not contractual ones.”3 35
  5. Visible strengths. A $90M Series C on September 15, 2022, led by Goldman Sachs Asset Management with Intel Capital, Foundation Capital, Neotribe, and In-Q-Tel (the CIA-linked strategic investor), bringing the total raised to over $122M.36 “522%” three-year growth and a 2022 Inc. 5000 listing. Named customers including Intel, Google, IBM, Goldman Sachs, VMware, Equinix, UCSF/BeeKeeperAI, NEC, and ElevenLabs. Footer attestations: SOC, ISO, PCI DSS, FIPS.
  6. Visible weaknesses. Armet AI (“Industry-first turnkey Agentic AI platform”) is the closest Fortanix gets to the agent-governance lane, offering “Built-in AI guardrails and governance,” “Universal Policy Enforcement,” RBAC, and “immutable audit logs.” That governance is scoped to the pipeline, the project, and the role, one level above the individual action, and the accountability artifact is an immutable audit log that stops short of a tamper-evident per-action receipt a regulator can independently verify.68 The attestation gate operates on workload and key release rather than on agent actions: “Access to datasets and model artifacts is allowed only after verified attestation.”69 The Confidential AI thesis is to bring the model into the customer’s own TEE (“on their own servers, on their own data”), which protects the model the operator controls and does not govern a third-party hosted closed model at the call boundary.35
  7. Recent dated activity. A March 18, 2026 release, “Fortanix Confidential AI Protects Proprietary Model IP and Data for Secure AI Inference in Enterprise AI Factories,” on NVIDIA Confidential Computing, naming ElevenLabs, “Available immediately.”3 An October 2025 release bringing Armet AI to NVIDIA Confidential Computing GPUs, and an April 2025 release launching Armet AI.70
  8. Overlap with Sealed. Fortanix is the most vocabulary-adjacent competitor in the set: enclave, TEE, attestation, key release, “cryptographic guarantees, not contractual ones,” immutable audit logs, sovereignty. The overlap is precisely why the layer matters. Fortanix applies the vocabulary to confidentiality of data and model; Sealed applies it to accountability of the agent’s actions.

The open lane next to Fortanix: Fortanix’s four March 2026 assurances are all about confidentiality and integrity of keys, weights, memory, and environment (“Cryptographic keys are released only to verified, trusted runtimes; Model weights remain confidential and tamper-proof; Customer prompts and outputs are encrypted in memory; Deployment environments can’t be altered without detection”).71 Not one says a specific agent action was authorized and is independently provable. Fortanix proves, with cryptography, that the right model ran in the right enclave on protected data. It produces no per-action gate chain and no independently regulator-verifiable receipt for each thing an agent actually did. Its strongest accountability artifact is an immutable audit log, which is the exact layer Sealed occupies above Fortanix’s cryptographic substrate.

3. Whitespace Map

Figure 2. Each dot is a competitor; dot size reflects market presence. The horizontal axis is how well a vendor secures where the model runs and the data lives; the vertical axis is how far it governs and proves what the AI does, per action. The rust-shaded top-right quadrant (sealed execution paired with a regulator-forwardable per-action receipt) is uncontested, and is the lane Sealed occupies.

Five lanes sit next to this competitor set. Each is marked genuinely open or apparently open, with the reason any apparent lane stays unclaimed.

Lane 1: the hardware-signed, regulator-forwardable per-action receipt across the closed-model boundary. Genuinely open. No named competitor produces it on public evidence. Guardrail vendors emit self-asserted console logs (HiddenLayer’s “evidence,” Protect AI’s “audit trail,” Lakera’s API verdict).21 16 11 Confidential-compute vendors attest where the model runs and cannot enclave a closed external model (Anjuna, Fortanix).30 35 Azure ships the cryptographic primitive as a build-it-yourself store.26 The lane is structurally open because the gap is architectural, not a positioning choice a rival can flip overnight.

Lane 2: governing the closed third-party model rather than refusing it. Genuinely open. Lakera screens text beside a closed model but does not attest its actions.12 Anjuna and Fortanix require the model to run inside a TEE the operator controls, so a hosted closed model falls outside the trust boundary.30 35 The “govern, don’t refuse, the closed model” lane is uncontested across both camps.

Lane 3: per-action governance as the product, above raw confidential compute. Apparently open, with a reason. Anjuna’s Overwatch and Fortanix’s Armet AI both reach toward this rhetorically. The reason the lane remains open is that both ship the governance as a side effect of isolation (enclave identity, pipeline policy), and Anjuna’s Overwatch has no live case study at all.29 68 The risk to track: a confidential-compute vendor with a real TEE stack is one shipped product away from contesting this lane, so the moat is the receipt artifact, not the words.

Lane 4: a proof artifact that outlives the vendor. Genuinely open. Every competitor’s proof lives in the vendor’s own console or policy manager (HiddenLayer, Anjuna, Protect AI), or terminates inside the single vendor (Azure’s abuse-monitoring access).21 66 62 A record a regulator can replay without contacting the company, and that survives the vendor’s disappearance, is unoccupied.

Lane 5: claims-discipline as a posture. Apparently open, with a reason. HiddenLayer’s blog already argues “trust requires verification,” and Fortanix already sells “cryptographic guarantees, not contractual ones.”59 3 The language of restraint is being adopted. The lane stays open because the discipline competitors claim is undercut by the proof they ship: a console log, an immutable audit log, a contractual commitment. Sealed’s posture (state what it cannot prove, hand the buyer a way to check it) is occupied only if Sealed’s verifier is reachable on the buyer surface, which today it is not (ARG-2).

4. TCO Comparison Logic

No aggregate price table exists for this set, because the published terms do not support one and several competitors price by negotiated enterprise contract. What follows is the structure a buyer should price against, using only individually sourced facts.

  1. The sticker hides the floor. Guardrail products market low switching cost as a feature. Lakera “deploys in minutes, requires no changes to your models or prompts.”38 That lowers the entry cost and raises the exit ease, so the comparison a buyer should run looks past first-year license to the cost of the proof gap: a guardrail’s output is a probabilistic verdict, so the buyer still funds whatever produces audit-grade evidence separately.
  2. The platform bundle adds estate cost. Azure’s confidential-AI posture pulls real adjacent costs. Purview DSPM for AI is gated to “Microsoft 365 E5 and E5 Compliance licenses.”63 Default confidential-VM key custody is Microsoft-managed; moving to customer-managed keys is the buyer’s opt-in.61 And encrypted OS disks for confidential VMs incur higher costs from March 30, 2026 per Microsoft’s own note.4 A buyer pricing “Azure equals Sealed” must add the E5 estate, the customer-managed-key work, and the cost of building the per-action audit trail Microsoft’s shared-responsibility doc assigns to them.27
  3. The confidential-compute lock-in is the durable cost. Fortanix’s Switching Cost runs through key and HSM custody: once DSM holds the keys with FIPS 140-2 Level 3 hardware and external key stores wired into AWS, GCP, and Azure, migration is painful.34 That is a moat for Fortanix and a cost a buyer should weigh before standardizing on it.

The TCO worksheet a rep completes live (do not pre-compute; fill with the prospect’s real numbers and the competitor’s published terms):

Line item Prospect input Competitor’s published term
Seats / agents under governance [fill: count] [fill from competitor quote]
License or platform floor [fill] [fill: e.g., M365 E5 for Purview DSPM]63
Required add-ons [fill] [fill: e.g., customer-managed keys on Azure]61
Cost to produce regulator-grade proof [fill] [fill: build-it-yourself on Confidential Ledger]26
Exit / migration cost [fill] [fill: low for guardrails38, high for key custody34]

5. Migration Friction Map and Anti-Switching Triggers

What it costs a buyer to leave each incumbent, and the live phrases a rep listens for. Each listener cue is anchored to a real customer-review verbatim captured in the research corpus; the cue is the signal, the quote is its source.

Leaving a guardrail vendor (Lakera, Protect AI, HiddenLayer): low friction, fast trigger. Guardrails are API filters and scanners with weak switching costs by their own marketing.38 The trigger is a forcing event the filter cannot answer with proof. Listener cues, each drawn from a real verbatim: “I need an audit trail for compliance, and a screenshot of a dashboard isn’t it” (anchored to a builder describing his own LLM firewall: “Everything gets logged with full context. Exportable audit trail for compliance”72); “the security review keeps coming back with the same Excel sheet of questions” (anchored to “they’ll throw big Excel sheets at you with questions”73); “we can detect the leak, but I can’t prove what the model actually did with the data” (anchored to “A distributed agentic AI with provenance and audit trails… is a more defensible architecture than a single end-to-end chatbot for high-risk domains”74).

Leaving Microsoft Purview / Azure: high friction, frustration-driven trigger. The Azure estate is sticky (tenant, Entra, E5). The trigger is the lived gap between what Purview promises and what it does. Listener cues, each from a real Purview review: “Purview hasn’t reduced the number of tools we’re stitching together” (verbatim: “Purview hasn’t come far enough yet to help us reduce the number of solutions that interact with each other”75); “the feature is in the console but it won’t work without yet another license” (verbatim: “Licensing is frustrating due to multiple modules and licenses. The feature might be available in the console, but it won’t work without the proper license”76); “the data lineage across our systems is incomplete” (verbatim: “There are some limitations to the solution with regard to the lineage of data from different systems”77).

Leaving a confidential-compute vendor (Anjuna, Fortanix): high friction, capability-gap trigger. Key custody and enclave wrapping create real lock-in.34 The trigger fires when the buyer discovers that isolation does not produce the per-action proof a regulator wants, well after the isolation itself is working fine. Listener cues, anchored to the regulated-data corpus: “we proved the model ran in the enclave, but the auditor wanted proof of each action it took” (anchored to the demand for “audit trails,” “provenance,” and “a logged boundary” around agent actions across the corpus74); “the platform is powerful but it takes a dedicated specialist to get value out of it” (verbatim, on a governance platform: “The platform does not reveal its secrets easily. You will be rewarded if you can allocate a team member to specialize on the platform”78); “we can’t put the closed model we actually use inside our own enclave” (anchored to the privilege-and-confidentiality concern: client data “stored and hosted on a third party service… may have to turn over client data in response to subpoenas”79).

6. Compliance Posture

The category operates under a basket of active, sequenced forcing events, not a single deadline. The durable demand story anchors on the active, non-slipping US federal and sectoral triggers, because every clean single date is fluid.

The empirical backdrop. IBM’s 2025 Cost of a Data Breach Report found 13% of organizations reported a breach of an AI model or application, and 97% of those lacked proper AI access controls; 63% had no AI governance policy at all.80 Gartner forecasts that “through 2026, at least 80% of unauthorized AI transactions will be caused by internal violations of enterprise policies… rather than malicious attacks,” which moves the threat model toward per-action policy enforcement.81 Deloitte’s 2026 State of AI found only about 21% of companies report a mature governance model for autonomous AI agents, even though 73% cite data privacy and security as their top AI risk.82

  • The active, non-slipping triggers (the basket). SEC Regulation S-P amendments require a written incident-response program and customer notification within 30 days of discovering unauthorized access; larger entities had to comply by December 3, 2025, and smaller entities by June 3, 2026.83 CMMC Phase 2 begins November 10, 2026, ending self-attestation for the Level 2 defense-contractor population and requiring independent C3PAO certification of all 110 NIST SP 800-171 controls as a condition of award.84 The NAIC Model Bulletin on insurers’ use of AI has now been adopted by roughly 24 to 25 states plus Washington, D.C., still growing.85 The HIPAA Security Rule NPRM (January 6, 2025) would make most implementation specifications mandatory, with an expected final-rule timeframe around May 2026.86 And on May 11, 2026, CB Financial Services filed what counsel describe as the first SEC Form 8-K triggered by unauthorized “shadow AI” use, after an employee processed customer names, Social Security numbers, and dates of birth in an unauthorized AI app.87
  1. The fluid dates the pitch must not lean on. The EU AI Act high-risk obligations slipped again under the May 2026 Digital Omnibus to December 2, 2027 (Annex III) and August 2, 2028 (Annex I), a second slip.88 The Colorado AI Act was delayed to January 1, 2027.89 NYDFS confirmed it imposes no new AI rules, enforcing AI risk through existing cyber regulation.90 A demand narrative built on any single fixed date is fragile; the basket is durable.
  2. The proof bar is rising past the badge. Independent 2026 compliance sources corroborate that SOC 2 is “necessary but not sufficient” for AI workloads, that “the core issue isn’t designing controls, it’s proving they work” at agent action-frequency where “auditors treat ‘no human request’ as a major accountability gap,” and that some platforms now provide “hardware-signed attestations” to “verify it mathematically with each interaction.”91
  3. Auditable-specifics checklist. A punch list, not a vibe. Anything not confirmed in the record is tagged [client to confirm].
Attestation / proof shipped (or in motion) Attestation the named ICP will look for
Parent DCP SOC 2 Type 1, third-party examined, announced Nov 13, 20257 A Sealed-named SOC 2 Type II, independently verifiable in Sealed’s own name [client to confirm]
Parent DCP SOC 2 Type 2 examination initiated; FedRAMP Moderate targeted for 20267 FedRAMP authorization for the government and defense ICP [client to confirm]
Six parent Cloud-OS patent applications filed, Sep 20258 Sealed-specific patents granted (today filed, not granted) [client to confirm]
Hardware-signed per-action receipt and public verifier (documented under the NexArt brand) The verifier reachable on the buyer-facing Sealed surface (today off-surface, ARG-2) [client to confirm]
CMMC Level 2 readiness for the defense segment [client to confirm] C3PAO Level 2 certification before the Nov 10, 2026 Phase 2 gate84

7. Vulnerability Watch

The forensic catches lead this section, because a live placeholder or a dead CTA on a security vendor’s own site is a head-to-head talking point a rep can use today. The structural exposures follow, where the gap is architectural and durable.

7.1 Lakera ships placeholder text and dead CTAs on its security page

The Vulnerability. A vendor whose entire pitch is rigor and precision ships template boilerplate and broken links on the very page that sells its trustworthiness.

The Evidence. > The /security page renders the Webflow filler string “This is some text inside of a div block.” thirteen times. The homepage includes a literal placeholder.svg asset and a “youtube placeholder.avif” demo thumbnail. Footer CTAs for Pricing, Cookies, Imprint, and Insights all point to a dead “#” anchor. The cookie banner contains uncorrected typos: “strictly necessaryfor,” “webstites,” “Preferencee,” “infomartion.”

Source: https://www.lakera.ai/security and https://www.lakera.ai/ (fetched 2026-06-18).92

The Window. Closing. The Check Point acquisition will likely fund a site cleanup, so the artifact is freshest now. The durable point underneath survives the cleanup: thin compliance surface (SOC 2 and GDPR only, no per-action runtime artifact).42

Recommended Response. In a head-to-head, a rep says: “Their security page shipped placeholder template text thirteen times and four dead navigation links. Look at the rigor on the page that is supposed to prove rigor. Now ask them for a hardware-signed receipt for a single agent action, and watch where the conversation goes.”

7.2 Protect AI runs a zombie funnel with duplicated H1s and a stale feed

The Vulnerability. Post-acquisition, the standalone site is in maintenance limbo while still soliciting demos, and it renders CMS artifacts that undercut the “most comprehensive platform” claim.

The Evidence. > Product pages render duplicated H1s (“# Guardian Guardian” and “# Layer Layer”). The Layer homepage card image filename is misspelled “Layour.svg.” Scan-count claims are inconsistent across the same site (Guardian page says “over 1.5 million” Hugging Face models while the homepage counter says “4,840,000+”). The footer mislabels an “NB Defense” link that actually points to huntr.com, and the footer logo is an un-renamed “unnamed-2.png.” The “Explore the Latest” feed surfaces posts dated January to April 2025.

Source: https://protectai.com/ , https://protectai.com/guardian , https://protectai.com/layer (live, 2026-06-18).48

The Window. Open and likely to stay open while the products migrate into Prisma AIRS and the standalone site is deprioritized. The buyer-facing question is which product they are actually buying.

Recommended Response. A rep says: “Their own site reports three different scan counts and runs a demo funnel for products their parent says are no longer sold separately. If the marketing surface is that ambiguous about what you are buying, ask them what your auditor receives at the end of an agent action. Their answer is a log in their console. Ours is a receipt your regulator can verify without calling us.”

7.3 HiddenLayer publishes the argument against its own evidence model

The Vulnerability. HiddenLayer’s own thought-leadership concedes that detection without evidence shifts the burden downstream, then defines its evidence as unsigned, console-resident software context.

The Evidence. > “Detection without evidence does not reduce risk, it shifts the burden downstream.” HiddenLayer defines its evidence as “the exact function calls associated with a detection, the arguments passed into those functions, the configurations that indicate anomalous or malicious behavior.” Its NSPM-11 blog argues “trust requires verification” and assurance must “operate independently of any single model vendor,” yet the verifier offered is its own console.

Source: https://www.hiddenlayer.com/insight/from-detection-to-evidence-making-ai-security-actionable-in-real-time and the NSPM-11 insight (fetched 2026-06-18).21 59

The Window. Open and durable. The gap is architectural: an agentless, observe-from-outside design cannot produce a hardware-signed artifact without a redesign.

Recommended Response. A rep says: “HiddenLayer wrote that detection without evidence just shifts the burden downstream, and that trust requires verification independent of any single vendor. Take them at their word. Their evidence is unsigned and lives in their console, and the verifier is their own tool. We produce the hardware-signed receipt their own blog says you need, in a public log neither of us controls.”

7.4 Azure documents in its own pages that governance is the buyer’s job

The Vulnerability. Microsoft’s first-party shared-responsibility documentation assigns the AI application safety system, orchestration inspection, and accountability for AI output to the customer, and concentrates the trust boundary inside Microsoft by default.

The Evidence. > “An application safety system must be built to protect the AI application from malicious activities. The safety system… inspects the interactions with any plugins, data connectors, and other AI applications (known as AI Orchestration).” On abuse monitoring: “The human reviewers are authorized Microsoft employees who access the data via point wise queries… and Just-In-Time (JIT) request approval.” On keys: “Microsoft can own or manage encryption keys for increased convenience.”

Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/security/fundamentals/shared-responsibility-ai and the Foundry data-privacy and confidential-VM docs (accessed 2026-06-18).27 62 61

The Window. Open and structural. The shared-responsibility model is foundational to the hyperscaler architecture, so the admission is durable.

Recommended Response. A rep says: “Azure’s own documentation says the AI application safety system must be built by you, that inspecting plugin and orchestration calls is your job, and that by default Microsoft staff can access a sample of your prompts and Microsoft can hold your keys. They give you the hardware and a contract. We give you the per-action receipt across the closed-model boundary that Microsoft tells you to build yourself, and you do not have to trust us to verify it.”

7.5 Anjuna positions Overwatch ahead of any shipped proof

The Vulnerability. Anjuna’s front door now sells an “incorruptible supervisor” for agents, but the named product has no live page, no datasheet, and no case study.

The Evidence. > Anjuna Overwatch is described as a product that “governs autonomous agents in real time” and “verifies every request through hardware-rooted attestation.” The agentic-AI page ships no per-action receipt, no external verifiable log, and reads “No current matching case studies. Check back soon.” Anjuna attests code at boot (“verifying that a piece of code is exactly what it claims to be… before any secrets are released”), not each action against an external model.

Source: https://www.anjuna.io/solution/agentic-ai (fetched 2026-06-18).29 30

The Window. Open now, and the rep should treat it as present-tense fact only: today there is no shipped agent-governance case study and no receipt artifact. State what is on the page; claim nothing about what Anjuna will do next.

Recommended Response. A rep says: “Anjuna’s homepage says agents need a supervisor and names Overwatch. Ask to see the per-action receipt and the case study. Today the page says no case studies are available, and the proof is attestation of code at boot, verified against Anjuna’s own policy manager. We ship the per-action receipt and a public verifier, and a closed model like GPT-4o that Anjuna cannot put in its enclave still gets governed at the boundary.”

7.6 Fortanix proves the data and logs the action

The Vulnerability. Fortanix’s strongest proof claims are all about confidentiality and integrity of keys, weights, memory, and environment; its accountability artifact for agent actions is an immutable audit log.

The Evidence. > The four March 2026 assurances: “Cryptographic keys are released only to verified, trusted runtimes; Model weights remain confidential and tamper-proof; Customer prompts and outputs are encrypted in memory; Deployment environments can’t be altered without detection.” Armet AI offers “Universal Policy Enforcement” and “immutable audit logs,” pipeline-scoped and role-scoped, not per-action.

Source: https://www.fortanix.com/company/pr/2026/03/… and https://www.fortanix.com/platform/armet-ai (fetched 2026-06-18).71 68

The Window. Open and durable, because the proof unit differs by design: Fortanix proves “the right runtime ran,” not “this specific action was authorized and is independently provable.”

Recommended Response. A rep says: “Fortanix says cryptographic guarantees, not contractual ones, and they mean it for keys, weights, and memory. Read their four assurances back: none of them says a specific agent action was authorized and is provable. Their accountability artifact is an immutable audit log in their environment. Ours is a per-action receipt your regulator replays without contacting either of us, and it works on the closed third-party model Fortanix asks you to bring into your own enclave.”

8. Cliche Saturation Index

A phrase qualifies when three or more competitors use it, each occurrence sourced to a competitor surface. If Sealed currently uses a saturated phrase, the row is flagged “must retire.”

Phrase Competitors who use it Recommendation for Sealed
“most comprehensive” / “broadest” AI security Protect AI (“broadest and most comprehensive”)43, HiddenLayer (“most comprehensive”)19 Differentiate. Decline the superlative race; claim the specific artifact rivals lack.
“audit trail” / “immutable audit log” (as a self-asserted log) Protect AI (“comprehensive audit trail”)16, Fortanix (“immutable audit logs”)68, HiddenLayer (console “evidence”)21 Must retire as a generic claim. Rename Sealed’s output the per-action receipt and define it: hardware-signed, in a public log.
“real-time” / “runtime” governance Lakera37, Protect AI (“at runtime”)14, HiddenLayer (“in Real Time”)52, Anjuna (“at runtime”)1 Differentiate. Pair “per action” with “independently verifiable,” which the others cannot say.
“guardrails” Lakera2, Protect AI (LLM Guard)17, HiddenLayer (“AI Guardrails & Firewall”)56, Fortanix (“AI Guardrails”)68 Differentiate. Sealed governs and proves; do not lead on the guardrail word the gateway camp owns.
“enterprise-grade” / “trust” / “trusted” Protect AI (“Enterprise Scale”)93, Anjuna (“trusted environments”)1, Fortanix (“trust, security and sovereignty”)32 Retire as a differentiator. “Trust” is the word; the receipt is the proof. State the proof.
“govern what the AI does” / “control what it does” Lakera (“Control what it does”)2, Anjuna (“supervisor”)1, HiddenLayer (“governance”)51 Must retire as the lead line. Rivals now own this phrase; lead on the receipt artifact (REC-001).

9. Battle Cards

One card per competitor. Each Defense Line shows only the spoken response, in a short (call) register and a long (email) register, each built on a publicly verifiable competitor fact. The case-math block carries the most relevant proof point and a worksheet the rep fills live. Sealed is design-partner stage, so client-proof points are tagged [client to confirm] rather than asserted.

9.1 Card: Lakera (a Check Point company)

30-second positioning. Lakera is the recognizable name in prompt-injection defense, with a real data moat in the Gandalf corpus, now inside Check Point. It screens text at an API boundary and issues a probabilistic verdict. It runs no model in a sealed enclave and produces no hardware-signed per-action receipt.

Top 3 attack lines. 1. “Lakera’s own security page says it screens your inputs and outputs ‘via our API’ using ‘business logic.’ That is a filter beside the model, and the model still runs unprotected wherever it ran before.” 2. “Their numbers admit a miss rate: ‘0.01% false positive rate,’ ‘98 percent’ detection. An ML classifier gives you an allow-or-block verdict, not proof of what the model did.” 3. “Lakera markets that it ‘deploys in minutes, requires no changes to your models.’ Easy to install means easy to rip out, and it means no data gravity holding an auditor’s evidence.”

Top 3 defense lines (spoken response only).

  • Short (call): “Lakera owns the prompt-injection data, and that is real. It is detection of attacks at the text boundary; we issue a hardware-signed receipt for each action, which their API verdict structurally cannot produce.” Long (email): “Lakera’s strongest asset is the Gandalf corpus, 80 million-plus adversarial patterns that genuinely improve prompt-attack detection, credited in the Check Point acquisition release. That moat covers detecting malicious prompts. It confers no advantage in attestation or a per-action proof artifact. Where the security review asks you to prove what the model did on a given action and hand that proof to a regulator, Lakera’s API verdict has no answer and we do.”
  • Short (call): “They say ‘control what it does,’ same as everyone now. The difference is the receipt: ask them to produce one a regulator can verify without calling Lakera.” Long (email): “Lakera’s banner reads ‘Controlling what AI can access isn’t enough. Control what it does,’ which is the exact governance language the whole category has converged on. Because the words are identical, the only honest comparison is the artifact. Lakera produces a screening verdict backed by business logic. We produce a hardware-signed, tamper-evident receipt per action, published to a public log that a regulator can replay independently.”
  • Short (call): “Their live compliance surface is SOC 2 and GDPR, both company-process attestations, with no runtime evidence per action.” Long (email): “On Lakera’s security page the attestations shown are SOC 2 and GDPR. Those describe Lakera the company’s process. They are not runtime evidence of any individual AI action. The granular layer a regulated buyer increasingly asks for, a provable record of each action, is the layer our per-action receipt provides and their certification badges do not.”

Key proof points. Acquired by Check Point, announced September 16, 2025 (not Cisco).13 Gandalf reports “80M+ Total Prompts.”10 Stated “0.01% production false positive rate.”41

Case math for this deal. Most relevant proof point: [client to confirm] a regulated design-partner reference where the per-action receipt cleared a security review a guardrail’s log could not. Worksheet: count the agent actions per month that touch regulated data; for each, ask what artifact Lakera’s API verdict leaves for the auditor; price the separate tooling the buyer funds today to turn a verdict into audit evidence.

9.2 Card: Protect AI (Prisma AIRS)

30-second positioning. Protect AI built the MLSecOps category and a real threat-intel flywheel (huntr, Hugging Face), now owned by Palo Alto Networks and folded into Prisma AIRS. The stack scans, red-teams, and monitors. There is no confidential computing, no sealed enclave, and the “audit trail” is a log in Protect AI’s own console.

Top 3 attack lines. 1. “Every Protect AI product is software interception: file scanning, red-team attacks, eBPF traffic monitoring. There is no hardware root of trust anywhere in the stack.” 2. “Guardian markets a ‘comprehensive audit trail… in your centralized Guardian environment.’ That is a log inside their console, not something a regulator can verify without trusting Protect AI.” 3. “Their own site reports three different scan counts (1.5 million, 4 million, 4.84 million) and runs a demo funnel for products the parent says are no longer sold separately.”

Top 3 defense lines (spoken response only).

  • Short (call): “Protect AI’s threat-intel community is genuinely strong, and it is intel, not proof of what your AI did per action.” Long (email): “Protect AI’s most defensible asset is the huntr community of 17,000-plus researchers and the Hugging Face scanning relationship, a real threat-intel flywheel now inside Palo Alto. That tells you about known attacks. It does not produce a hardware-attested record of each action your agent took. When the auditor asks you to prove a specific action across the closed-model boundary, scanning and monitoring leave a console log, and we leave a forwardable receipt.”
  • Short (call): “Their best government quote, from Leidos, sells coverage breadth, and never claims proof of what the AI did per action.” Long (email): “Protect AI’s strongest public reference is Leidos framing the win as ‘from the model file to runtime behavior, a holistic, platform-based approach.’ That is breadth. In the regulated and national-security contexts Leidos serves, the unanswered question is whether you can prove, hardware-rooted, what the AI did on each action and forward that proof to a regulator. Their stack answers with logs. Ours answers with a receipt.”
  • Short (call): “Detection means an irreducible bypass surface. Layer is ML classifiers with a guaranteed evasion gap; we gate and receipt the action itself.” Long (email): “Layer relies on ‘27 turnkey policies based on 15 different security scanners.’ Classifier-based detection has a permanent false-negative surface, which is why it is probabilistic, not provable. We do not bet the audit on catching an attack. We gate each action and emit a signed receipt of the decision, which is provable regardless of whether a novel attack evaded a classifier.”

Key proof points. Acquired by Palo Alto Networks, completed July 22, 2025.18 huntr community “17,000+” researchers.15 Guardian “audit trail… in your centralized Guardian environment.”16

Case math for this deal. Most relevant proof point: [client to confirm] a regulated reference where the forwardable receipt cleared a review that a scanner’s log stalled. Worksheet: list the model files scanned and runtime endpoints monitored; for each, note what Protect AI hands the auditor (a console log); price the gap between that log and a regulator-replayable receipt.

9.3 Card: HiddenLayer

30-second positioning. HiddenLayer is a purpose-built AI detection-and-response vendor with real research IP (30-plus patents, 50-plus CVEs) and a deliberately agentless design that observes from outside the model. It cannot run the model in a sealed enclave, and its evidence is unsigned software context in its own console.

Top 3 attack lines. 1. “HiddenLayer’s whole design is agentless: ‘without ever needing to access weights, prompts, or customer data.’ It observes from outside the inference path, so it cannot attest what happens inside a closed model.” 2. “Their own blog says ‘detection without evidence… shifts the burden downstream,’ then defines their evidence as function calls and configurations in their console. That is exactly the unsigned, vendor-held proof they warn about.” 3. “Their runtime product is an ‘AI Guardrails & Firewall’ that blocks malicious prompts. That is software-gateway screening that emits alerts, with no signed record of the governed action.”

Top 3 defense lines (spoken response only).

  • Short (call): “Their research is real, and research is not a receipt. Their evidence is unsigned and lives in their console; ours is hardware-signed in a public log.” Long (email): “HiddenLayer’s Cornered Resource is genuine: 30-plus issued patents and 50-plus disclosed CVEs from a strong threat-research team. That makes their detectors good. It does not make their evidence verifiable. Their own ‘From Detection to Evidence’ insight defines evidence as console-resident function calls and configurations, with no signature and no independent log. We issue a hardware-signed receipt per action that a regulator replays without contacting either vendor.”
  • Short (call): “They wrote that trust requires verification independent of any single vendor, then made themselves the verifier. We made a public log the verifier.” Long (email): “HiddenLayer’s June 2026 NSPM-11 blog argues that assurance must ‘operate independently of any single model vendor’ and that ‘trust requires verification.’ We agree, and we built to it: the verifier is a public transparency log, not our own console. HiddenLayer’s verifier is its own tooling, which is the single-vendor dependency its own blog argues against.”
  • Short (call): “Agentless is their selling point and their ceiling. They never run the model, so they cannot seal it or prove an action across the closed-model boundary.” Long (email): “HiddenLayer markets ‘Model-Agnostic, Agentless, Zero Training Data Required.’ That lowers friction and it caps the proof. Because they never execute the model, they cannot run it in a sealed enclave and cannot attest an action crossing into a closed third-party model. That boundary is exactly where a regulated buyer needs proof, and it is the boundary our receipt covers.”

Key proof points. $50M Series A, September 19, 2023, led by M12 and Moore Strategic Ventures.23 “Detection without evidence does not reduce risk, it shifts the burden downstream.”21 Compliance footprint on-site is SOC 2 only.57

Case math for this deal. Most relevant proof point: [client to confirm] a federal or regulated reference where a signed, public-log receipt satisfied an auditor that console alert-context did not. Worksheet: count the agent actions touching the closed model; note what HiddenLayer’s console holds for each (unsigned context); price what it costs to make that context independently verifiable, which is the gap the receipt closes.

9.4 Card: Microsoft Azure

30-second positioning. Azure offers hardware data-in-use isolation, platform attestation, a contractual AI-privacy promise, and a cryptographic-receipt primitive in Confidential Ledger. By Microsoft’s own shared-responsibility documentation, per-action agent governance and a vendor-independent audit trail across the closed-model boundary are the buyer’s to build.

Top 3 attack lines. 1. “Azure’s own AI shared-responsibility doc says the ‘application safety system must be built’ by you, and inspecting plugin and orchestration calls is your job.” 2. “By default Microsoft can hold your keys ‘for increased convenience,’ and abuse monitoring lets ‘authorized Microsoft employees’ access a sample of your prompts and completions. The trust boundary terminates inside Microsoft.” 3. “Confidential Ledger mints Merkle receipts, and it is a general-purpose store you must wire to your agent’s actions and design the regulator-forwarding around. The primitive exists; the governed-action product does not.”

Top 3 defense lines (spoken response only).

  • Short (call): “Azure’s scale and isolation are real, and Microsoft documents that the governance layer is yours to build. We ship that layer.” Long (email): “Azure’s Scale, Switching Costs, and Branding are formidable and real for infrastructure. They do not extend to per-action governance: Microsoft’s own shared-responsibility doc states the AI application safety system ‘must be built’ by the customer and that orchestration inspection is the customer’s responsibility. We productize exactly that layer, vendor-independent, so you are not building it on Azure’s behalf.”
  • Short (call): “Their assurance is ‘trust Microsoft plus the contract.’ Ours is a receipt you verify without trusting either of us.” Long (email): “Azure’s data protection is a DPA-backed contractual commitment, reinforced by default abuse monitoring under which Microsoft employees can access sampled prompts and completions unless you are approved for modified monitoring. That is vendor-plus-contract trust. Our per-action receipt is hardware-signed and published to a public log, so a regulator confirms what happened independently of both Microsoft and us.”
  • Short (call): “Their attestation verifies the platform booted on SEV-SNP or TDX, not what an agent did across the closed-model boundary.” Long (email): “Azure confidential VMs boot ‘only after successful attestation of the platform’s critical components,’ which proves the hardware is a genuine TEE. It does not span the closed-model boundary or attest the content of an individual agent action. Our receipt attests both sides of that boundary per action, which platform attestation by design does not reach.”

Key proof points. “An application safety system must be built to protect the AI application.”27 Abuse monitoring: “authorized Microsoft employees… access the data.”62 Confidential Ledger receipts are a build-it-yourself store.26

Case math for this deal. Most relevant proof point: [client to confirm] a regulated cohort that chose vendor-independent receipts over building a Confidential Ledger pipeline. Worksheet: add the E5 estate cost for Purview DSPM, the customer-managed-key migration off the default Microsoft-held keys, and the engineering cost to wire Confidential Ledger to agent actions and build the regulator-forwarding mechanism; compare that build to a per-action receipt that ships with the governance.

9.5 Card: Anjuna

30-second positioning. Anjuna is a confidential-compute vendor with a strong cross-chip TEE stack and real government and bank references, now repositioning its front door onto agent governance with Overwatch. Overwatch ships no case study, no datasheet, and no per-action receipt. A closed external model cannot be enclaved by Anjuna.

Top 3 attack lines. 1. “Anjuna’s homepage says agents need a supervisor and names Overwatch. The agentic page reads ‘No current matching case studies. Check back soon.’ That is positioning ahead of proof.” 2. “Anjuna attests code at boot and enforces runtime policy, verified against its own policy manager inside your cloud. The unit of governance is the enclave, not the individual action, and there is no public verifiable log.” 3. “Their design requires the agent and model to run inside a TEE they control. A closed model like GPT-4o that you call by API cannot go inside Anjuna’s enclave, so the closed-model boundary falls outside their trust story.”

Top 3 defense lines (spoken response only).

  • Short (call): “Anjuna secures the room and attests the code at the door. It does not hand a regulator a replayable receipt for each action, which is our product.” Long (email): “Anjuna’s real strengths are its cross-chip TEE virtualization and its references, including the U.S. Navy and three of the world’s ten largest banks. Those prove where a workload runs. They do not prove what an agent did, action by action. Anjuna gates at boot and enforces policy against its own attestation manager; we emit a hardware-signed, append-only receipt per action that a regulator replays without contacting us.”
  • Short (call): “Their words match ours almost exactly, so judge the artifact. Theirs is attestation of code at boot; ours is a per-action receipt.” Long (email): “Anjuna’s vocabulary, supervisor, runtime policy, governing agent actions, is nearly identical to ours, which is precisely why the comparison has to be the artifact. Anjuna’s FAQ defines attestation as verifying ‘that a piece of code is exactly what it claims to be… before any secrets are released,’ which is code identity at boot. We govern and sign each individual action, and publish the receipt to a log neither vendor controls.”
  • Short (call): “Overwatch has no live page, no datasheet, and the case-study slot is empty. Ask to see one action governed end to end with a receipt.” Long (email): “On Anjuna’s own agentic-AI page, Overwatch appears as a paragraph with no product page, no datasheet, and a case-study section that reads ‘No items found.’ We are not speculating about their roadmap; we are reading their live page. Ask them to demonstrate a single agent action governed across a closed-model boundary with a regulator-forwardable receipt, the thing we ship today.”

Key proof points. Homepage H1: “Autonomous Agents Need a Supervisor.”1 Overwatch agentic page: “No current matching case studies.”29 “Now Trusted by Three of the World’s 10 Largest Banks,” June 11, 2025.65

Case math for this deal. Most relevant proof point: [client to confirm] a regulated reference where the per-action receipt governed a closed-model call Anjuna’s enclave could not contain. Worksheet: list the closed third-party models the buyer actually calls; mark which Anjuna can enclave (none it does not control); price the residual ungoverned boundary, which the receipt covers without enclaving the model.

9.6 Card: Fortanix

30-second positioning. Fortanix is the data-and-key security leader with durable key-custody lock-in and a real confidential-AI stack, expanding into agent governance with Armet AI. Its cryptographic guarantees secure keys, weights, memory, and environment. For agent actions, its accountability artifact reaches as far as an immutable audit log and stops short of a per-action receipt.

Top 3 attack lines. 1. “Fortanix says ‘cryptographic guarantees, not contractual ones,’ and their four assurances are all about keys, weights, memory, and environment. None says a specific agent action was authorized and is independently provable.” 2. “Armet AI’s governance is pipeline-scoped and role-scoped, with ‘immutable audit logs.’ That is tamper-resistant logging in their environment, not a regulator-replayable per-action receipt.” 3. “Their Confidential AI thesis is to bring the model into your own TEE. It protects the model you control and does not govern a third-party hosted closed model at the call boundary.”

Top 3 defense lines (spoken response only).

  • Short (call): “Fortanix proves the right model ran in the right enclave.
