We prove the right action happened, per action, and a regulator can check it.” Long (email): “Fortanix’s cryptographic guarantees are genuine and they secure the substrate: keys released only to verified runtimes, model weights tamper-proof, prompts encrypted in memory, environment unaltered. Every one is about confidentiality and integrity, which is a different unit of proof from accountability of action. We sit above that substrate and prove, per action, that the action was authorized, with a receipt a regulator replays independently.”
  • Short (call): “Their accountability artifact is an immutable audit log. Ours is a per-action receipt in a public log. Different units of proof.” Long (email): “Armet AI offers ‘Universal Policy Enforcement’ and ‘immutable audit logs.’ An immutable log is tamper-resistant and lives in Fortanix’s environment; it is not a tamper-evident, independently verifiable receipt for each individual agent action. Where the auditor wants a record per action that they can replay without trusting Fortanix, the log is self-asserted and our receipt is independently verifiable.”
  • Short (call): “Fortanix asks you to bring the model into your enclave. We govern the closed model you do not control, at the boundary, with proof.” Long (email): “Fortanix’s Confidential AI runs the model ‘on their own servers, on their own data,’ which works when you control the model. The closed third-party model you call by API cannot be brought into your enclave, so Fortanix does not govern that boundary. We govern the action across the closed-model boundary, attesting both sides without asserting what happens inside the model, and we emit the receipt.”

Key proof points. “Both parties operate with cryptographic guarantees, not contractual ones.”3 Armet AI: “immutable audit logs.”68 $90M Series C with In-Q-Tel participation, September 15, 2022.36

Case math for this deal. Most relevant proof point: [client to confirm] a regulated reference where the per-action receipt cleared an action-accountability requirement an immutable log did not satisfy. Worksheet: separate the data-and-key spend (where Fortanix is strong) from the action-governance requirement; for each closed-model call the buyer makes, note that Fortanix cannot enclave it; price the action-accountability layer the receipt provides above Fortanix’s substrate.

10. Strategic Implications

Each implication leads with the claim, anchors to evidence, and ends in an operative verb tied to a Brief recommendation.

  • Lead on the artifact, not the verb. The governance vocabulary has converged: Lakera, Anjuna, and HiddenLayer all say “control” or “govern what the AI does,” so the phrase no longer separates Sealed from anyone.2 1 51 Retire “govern what the AI does, action by action” as the lead line and make the headline claim the receipt a regulator verifies without contacting the company (REC-001). Reposition the message to the proof artifact.
  1. Move every competitive conversation to the receipt. Each rival cedes the same lane from a different angle: guardrails log, confidential-compute attests the platform, the hyperscaler assigns governance to the buyer.16 4 27 Build the standard demo around producing a hardware-signed per-action receipt across a closed-model boundary, the one motion no competitor matches, and run it in every head-to-head. Anchor the demo on the receipt.
  2. Surface Sealed’s proof where the buyer looks. The live verifier and disciplined governance story sit under the NexArt brand while the buyer-facing surface sells cost savings, so the best evidence is invisible during a security review (ARG-2).7 Move the verifier and the governance story onto the Sealed surface and give NexQloud Sealed a public identity. Migrate the proof to the buyer surface (REC-002).
  3. Separate Sealed’s credentials from the parent’s. The publicly verifiable SOC 2 is the parent DCP’s Type 1 and the announced patents are six parent Cloud-OS filings, filed not granted, so a buyer’s review finds the parent, not Sealed.7 8 Cleanly attribute which credential belongs to which product, and complete a Sealed-named SOC 2 Type II. Decouple the proof stack (REC-002).
  • Lead the demand story with the basket, not a date. The aggregate forcing function is strong and every clean single date is fluid: the EU high-risk deadline slipped twice, Colorado moved to 2027, NYDFS adds no new AI rules.88 89 90 Build the pitch on the active, non-slipping US federal and sectoral triggers (Reg S-P June 3, 2026; CMMC Phase 2 November 10, 2026; NAIC; the HIPAA NPRM; the first shadow-AI 8-K) mapped per vertical.83 84 87 Anchor the why-now on the basket (REC-005).

Treat SOC 2 as table stakes the receipt transcends. Customers themselves warn the checkbox is “a sales-enablement tool,” and 2026 compliance sources echo that SOC 2 is “necessary but not sufficient” and that buyers want to “verify it mathematically with each interaction.”91 Position the receipt as what survives the security review under a forcing event, and sell both ICPs (the regulated org and the stalled AI vendor) on the same forwardable-proof story (REC-006). Lead with provable governance, not the badge.

  • Reframe compliance as the gate, not a discount. The company’s own primary surface sells decentralized-cloud cost savings and frames compliance as a discount lever, which undercuts the governance-first thesis the market data rewards (attack surface up, governance maturity scarce).82 Rewrite the Sealed surface to lead governance-first and stop pricing compliance as a cost cut (REC-003). Reframe compliance as the right to serve.

Hold the claims-discipline voice on every surface. The disciplined NexArt voice states what it cannot prove, while the cost-savings surface leans on the hype conventions that voice rejects (vanity counters, a logo wall, near-identical testimonials) (ARG-4). Extend the auditor’s voice to the Sealed surface and retire the proof-theater scaffolding (REC-004). Standardize the voice on evidence over adjectives.

Name the proof gap plainly as the test to run. The architecture is structurally hard to copy and unproven: design-partner stage, no production cohort, patents filed not granted (BLF-011). State the gap openly and run the one test that closes it, a production regulated-cohort reference where a regulator accepts the forwarded per-action receipt governing a real closed-model call. Commission the production reference.

  • Lakera homepage (“Multimodal and model agnostic… across any model”), https://www.lakera.ai/ (fetched 2026-06-18).

  • Protect AI homepage and collateral (huntr “17,000+ security researchers,” MLSecOps “8,000+ members,” Hugging Face partnership), https://protectai.com/ (live, 2026-06-18).
  • Protect AI Guardian page (“a comprehensive audit trail of all evaluations in your centralized Guardian environment”), https://protectai.com/guardian (live, 2026-06-18).
  • Protect AI Guardian and Layer pages (file scanning, eBPF/SDK monitoring; LLM Guard guardrails), https://protectai.com/guardian and https://protectai.com/layer (live, 2026-06-18).

  • Microsoft Azure 7-Powers read (Scale, Switching Costs, Branding) per Azure confidential-computing and shared-responsibility surfaces, https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/solutions/confidential-compute (accessed 2026-06-18).

  • Lakera homepage differentiator stats (“Reduce risks by 3-4 orders of magnitude,” “sub-50 ms runtime latency,” “0.01% production false positive rate,” “100+ languages”), https://www.lakera.ai/ (fetched 2026-06-18).

  • Protect AI product pages (Guardian “35+ model formats”; Recon “450+ known attacks”; Layer “27 turnkey policies… 15 different security scanners,” eBPF/SDK), https://protectai.com/guardian , https://protectai.com/recon , https://protectai.com/layer (live, 2026-06-18).
  • Protect AI homepage and product pages (“most advanced AI security product suite,” “widest and deepest set of model scanners,” “attack patterns that competitors miss”), https://protectai.com/ , https://protectai.com/guardian , https://protectai.com/layer (live, 2026-06-18).
  • Protect AI funding (~$108.5M total; Seed $13.5M 2022-12-15; Series A $35M 2023-07-26; Series B $60M 2024-08-01) and acquisition value reported “north of $500 million,” via GeekWire/CNBC (2025) and Palo Alto Networks press release (2025-07-22), https://www.paloaltonetworks.com/company/press/2025/palo-alto-networks-completes-acquisition-of-protect-ai.
  • Protect AI Layer page (ML-classifier detection with an irreducible bypass surface; “27 turnkey policies”), https://protectai.com/layer (live, 2026-06-18).
  • Protect AI live-page forensic pass (duplicated H1s “# Guardian Guardian” / “# Layer Layer”; “Layour.svg”; inconsistent scan counts 1.5M/4M/4.84M; “NB Defense” link to huntr.com; “unnamed-2.png”; “Explore the Latest” feed dated Jan-Apr 2025), https://protectai.com/ , https://protectai.com/guardian , https://protectai.com/layer (live, 2026-06-18).
  • Protect AI Layer page (policies mapped to “NIST, MITRE, and OWASP”; “deep visibility and control”; “end-to-end”), https://protectai.com/layer (live, 2026-06-18).
  • Palo Alto Networks press release, Leidos quote (Eric Moore: “Securing AI, from the model file to runtime behavior… a holistic, platform-based approach… a single vendor to address end-to-end AI risk”), https://www.paloaltonetworks.com/company/press/2025/palo-alto-networks-completes-acquisition-of-protect-ai (2025-07-22).
  • HiddenLayer master positioning (“secures agentic, generative, and predictive AI applications across the entire lifecycle”; four modules), https://www.hiddenlayer.com/ and https://www.hiddenlayer.com/platform (fetched 2026-06-18).
  • HiddenLayer AI Runtime Security page (H1 “Detect and Stop AI Attacks in Real Time”; “protects agents from prompt attacks, jailbreaks, unsafe outputs, and malicious tool use”), https://www.hiddenlayer.com/platform/ai-runtime-security (fetched 2026-06-18).
  • HiddenLayer platform page (“Purpose-Built for AI Security… not retrofitted from traditional cybersecurity”), https://www.hiddenlayer.com/platform (fetched 2026-06-18).
  • HiddenLayer headcount (~167-169) and total raised (~$55.8M), https://pitchbook.com/profiles/company/501766-93 and https://tracxn.com/d/companies/hiddenlayer/ (accessed 2026-06-18).
  • HiddenLayer, “AISec Platform 2.0” launch at RSAC (Model Genealogy; AIBOM “an auditable inventory of model components, datasets, and dependencies”), https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/hiddenlayer-unveils-aisec-platform-2-0-to-deliver-unmatched-context-visibility-and-observability-for-enterprise-ai-security-302434498.html (2025-04-22).
  • HiddenLayer AI Runtime Security page (“AI Guardrails & Firewall… Enforce guardrails and block malicious prompts”; “Detect, redact, block, or redirect malicious activity immediately”), https://www.hiddenlayer.com/platform/ai-runtime-security (fetched 2026-06-18).
  • HiddenLayer security page (SOC 2 badges only; no FedRAMP or hardware attestation surfaced), https://www.hiddenlayer.com/security (fetched 2026-06-18).
  • HiddenLayer, “2026 AI Threat Landscape Report” and NSPM-11 insight (June 5, 2026), https://www.hiddenlayer.com/report-and-guide/threatreport2026 and https://www.hiddenlayer.com/insight/nspm-11-elevates-ai-security-from-best-practice-to-national-security-requirement (fetched 2026-06-18).
  • HiddenLayer NSPM-11 insight (“trust requires verification”; assurance must “operate independently of any single model vendor”), https://www.hiddenlayer.com/insight/nspm-11-elevates-ai-security-from-best-practice-to-national-security-requirement (fetched 2026-06-18).
  • NVIDIA blog (“Azure is the first cloud provider to offer confidential computing with NVIDIA H100 GPUs”) and Azure solutions page (“Specify the hardware and software that have access to your data and code, and verifiably enforce this guarantee”), https://blogs.nvidia.com/blog/azure-confidential-vm-h100-general-availability/ and https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/solutions/confidential-compute (accessed 2026-06-18).
  • Microsoft, confidential-VM overview (“Microsoft can own or manage encryption keys for increased convenience at no additional cost”), https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/confidential-computing/confidential-vm-overview (page last updated 2026-02-05; accessed 2026-06-18).
  • Microsoft, Foundry data-privacy doc on abuse monitoring (“The human reviewers are authorized Microsoft employees who access the data via point wise queries using request IDs, Secure Access Workstations (SAWs), and Just-In-Time (JIT) request approval”), https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/foundry/responsible-ai/openai/data-privacy (page last updated 2026-05-19; accessed 2026-06-18).
  • Microsoft, Purview DSPM for AI (agents as “first-class entities”; “available for customers with Microsoft 365 E5 and E5 Compliance licenses”), https://techcommunity.microsoft.com/blog/microsoft-security-blog/beyond-visibility-the-new-microsoft-purview-data-security-posture-management-dsp/4470984 and https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/purview/dspm-for-ai (accessed 2026-06-18).
  • Anjuna agentic-AI page, Overwatch description (“Establishes a trusted supervisor that governs autonomous agents in real time… verifies every request through hardware-rooted attestation”), https://www.anjuna.io/solution/agentic-ai (fetched 2026-06-18).
  • Anjuna press, “Now Trusted by Three of the World’s 10 Largest Banks” (2025-06-11) and U.S. Navy case study (Llama3 on confidential NVIDIA H100 GPUs), https://www.anjuna.io/press/confidential-computing-goes-mainstream-anjuna-now-trusted-by-three-of-the-worlds-10-largest-banks (2025-06-11) and https://www.anjuna.io/case-studies/united-states-navy.
  • Anjuna homepage FAQ and agentic-AI page (proof story is attestation of code identity at boot plus runtime policy enforcement against Anjuna’s own attestation policy manager; no externally published per-action record), https://www.anjuna.io/ and https://www.anjuna.io/solution/agentic-ai (fetched 2026-06-18).
  • Fortanix homepage, “Why Fortanix” (“the only data security provider that can truly say your data is under your control… full custody of your cloud keys”), https://www.fortanix.com/ (fetched 2026-06-18).
  • Fortanix Armet AI page (“Built-in AI guardrails and governance,” “Universal Policy Enforcement,” RBAC, “continuously monitor model outputs,” “immutable audit logs”), https://www.fortanix.com/platform/armet-ai (fetched 2026-06-18).
  • Fortanix Confidential AI page, “Secure Key Release” (“Access to datasets and model artifacts is allowed only after verified attestation. Decryption happens only within verified, isolated environments”), https://www.fortanix.com/platform/confidential-ai (fetched 2026-06-18).
  • Fortanix press releases: Armet AI on NVIDIA Confidential Computing GPUs (October 2025) and Armet AI launch (April 2025), https://www.fortanix.com/company/pr/2025/10/fortanix-brings-secure-and-trusted-agentic-ai-to-cutting-edge-nvidia-confidential-computing-gpus and https://www.fortanix.com/company/pr/2025/04/fortanix-armet-ai-enables-enterprises-to-create-secure-and-compliant-custom-gen-ai-applications-to-mitigate-data-exposure-risk.
  • Fortanix March 2026 release, four assurances (“Cryptographic keys are released only to verified, trusted runtimes; Model weights remain confidential and tamper-proof; Customer prompts and outputs are encrypted in memory; Deployment environments can’t be altered without detection”), https://www.fortanix.com/company/pr/2026/03/fortanix-confidential-ai-protects-proprietary-model-ip-and-data-for-secure-ai-inference-in-enterprise-ai-factories (2026-03-18).
  • Hacker News, comment on “Veto: Permission policy engine” (builder describing his LLM firewall: “Everything gets logged with full context. Exportable audit trail for compliance”).
  • Hacker News, comment on “Ask HN: As a new SaaS business…” (security review: “they’ll throw big Excel sheets at you with questions”).
  • Hacker News, comment on “OpenAI updates terms to forbid…” (“A distributed agentic AI with provenance and audit trails… is a more defensible architecture than a single end-to-end chatbot for high-risk domains”).
  • PeerSpot, Microsoft Purview Data Governance review (“Purview hasn’t come far enough yet to help us reduce the number of solutions that interact with each other”).
  • PeerSpot, Microsoft Purview Data Governance review (“Licensing is frustrating due to multiple modules and licenses. The feature might be available in the console, but it won’t work without the proper license”).
  • PeerSpot, Microsoft Purview Data Governance review (“There are some limitations to the solution with regard to the lineage of data from different systems”).
  • TrustRadius, OneTrust Privacy Automation review (“The platform does not reveal its secrets easily. You will be rewarded if you can allocate a team member to specialize on the platform”).
  • Hacker News, comment on “Mike: open-source legal AI” (law firms cannot host confidential or privileged client data on a third-party service that “may have to turn over client data in response to subpoenas”).
  • IBM Newsroom, “IBM Report: 13% Of Organizations Reported Breaches Of AI Models Or Applications, 97% Of Which Reported Lacking Proper AI Access Controls,” July 30, 2025, https://newsroom.ibm.com/2025-07-30-ibm-report-13-of-organizations-reported-breaches-of-ai-models-or-applications,-97-of-which-reported-lacking-proper-ai-access-controls.
  • Gartner AI TRiSM research (“Through 2026, at least 80% of unauthorized AI transactions will be caused by internal violations of enterprise policies… rather than malicious attacks”), via Mindgard analysis, https://mindgard.ai/blog/gartner-ai-trism-market-guide and Securiti, https://securiti.ai/what-is-ai-trism/.
  • Deloitte, “State of AI in the Enterprise 2026” (only ~21% report a mature governance model for autonomous AI agents; 73% cite data privacy and security as top AI risk), https://www.deloitte.com/us/en/insights/topics/emerging-technologies/ai-agents-scaling-faster.html and https://www.deloitte.com/us/en/what-we-do/capabilities/applied-artificial-intelligence/content/state-of-ai-in-the-enterprise.html.
  • U.S. SEC, Regulation S-P amendments (Press Release 2024-58); compliance deadlines (larger entities December 3, 2025; smaller entities June 3, 2026; 30-day notification), https://www.sec.gov/news/press-release/2024-58 ; Holland & Knight (May 2026), https://www.hklaw.com/en/insights/publications/2026/05/regulation-s-p-amendments-compliance-deadline-approaching.
  • U.S. DoD CIO, CMMC overview (Phase 2 begins November 10, 2026; C3PAO Level 2 certification of all 110 NIST SP 800-171 controls), https://dodcio.defense.gov/cmmc/about/ ; 48 CFR rule, Federal Register, Sept 10, 2025, https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2025/09/10/2025-17359/defense-federal-acquisition-regulation-assessing-contractor-implementation-of-cybersecurity.
  • NAIC Model Bulletin on the Use of AI Systems by Insurers (~24-25 states plus D.C., still growing), via Quarles, https://www.quarles.com/newsroom/publications/nearly-half-of-states-have-now-adopted-naic-model-bulletin-on-insurers-use-of-ai and NAIC implementation map, https://content.naic.org/sites/default/files/cmte-h-big-data-artificial-intelligence-wg-map-ai-model-bulletin.pdf.
  • HHS / Federal Register, “HIPAA Security Rule To Strengthen the Cybersecurity of Electronic Protected Health Information” (NPRM, Jan 6, 2025; expected final-rule timeframe ~May 2026), https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2025/01/06/2024-29999/hipaa-security-rule-to-strengthen-the-cybersecurity-of-electronic-protected-health-information.
  • Wilson Sonsini, “Shadow AI Triggers First SEC Form 8-K for Unauthorized AI Use” (CB Financial Services, filed May 11, 2026), https://www.wsgr.com/en/insights/shadow-ai-triggers-first-sec-form-8-k-for-unauthorized-ai-use-what-financial-institutions-and-public-companies-need-to-know.html ; corroborated by Intelligize, https://www.intelligize.com/shadow-ai-lands-on-the-secs-radar/.
  • EU AI Act high-risk obligations deferred under the May 2026 Digital Omnibus (Annex III to December 2, 2027; Annex I to August 2, 2028), via Gibson Dunn, https://www.gibsondunn.com/eu-ai-act-omnibus-agreement-postponed-high-risk-deadlines-and-other-key-changes/ ; Consilium press release (2026-05-07).
  • Colorado AI Act (SB 24-205) amended and delayed to January 1, 2027 (SB 26-189), https://leg.colorado.gov/bills/sb26-189 ; Hunton Andrews Kurth, https://www.huntonprivacyblog.com/2026/05/22/colorado-ai-act-amended-and-effective-date-delayed/.
  • NYDFS Industry Letter (Oct 16, 2024) using existing 23 NYCRR Part 500 to assess AI risk, imposing no new requirements; Frontier AI advisory (May 21, 2026), https://www.dfs.ny.gov/industry_guidance/industry_letters/il20241016_cyber_risks_ai and https://www.dfs.ny.gov/industry_guidance/industry_letters/il20260521_frontier_ai.
  • Independent 2026 security-engineering and compliance sources (SOC 2 “necessary but not sufficient”; “the core issue isn’t designing controls, it’s proving they work”; “auditors treat ‘no human request’ as a major accountability gap”; “hardware-signed attestations” to “verify it mathematically with each interaction”): Telnyx, “Are AI Voice Agents SOC 2 Compliant?”; Goteleport, “How AI Agents Impact SOC 2 Trust Services Criteria”; Blaxel, “SOC 2 Compliance for AI Agents in 2026”; Hicomply, “SOC 2 Compliance for AI Companies.”
  • Lakera live-page forensic pass (Webflow filler “This is some text inside of a div block.” x13; placeholder.svg; “youtube placeholder.avif”; dead “#” CTAs for Pricing, Cookies, Imprint, Insights; cookie-banner typos), https://www.lakera.ai/security and https://www.lakera.ai/ (fetched 2026-06-18).
  • Protect AI homepage / category framing (“Making AI Safe at Enterprise Scale”; “enterprise scale”), https://protectai.com/ (live, 2026-06-18).
